title: Shock — overview
oneLiner: Acute circulatory failure causing inadequate tissue oxygen delivery; life-threatening, common in all hospital settings, requires immediate recognition and cause-specific resuscitation.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic Vignette:**
A 68-year-old man admitted for pneumonia on Day 2 becomes confused, oliguric, and hypotensive (BP 82/45). He is tachycardic (HR 115), has cool, mottled knees, and a lactate of 5.1 mmol/L. His respiratory rate is 28, and his oxygen saturations are 88% on 4L. His JVP is low.

**Dissecting the vignette:**
- Confusion → reduced cerebral perfusion / metabolic acidosis  
- Oliguria → reduced renal perfusion pressure  
- Hypotension → loss of vascular tone +/or hypovolaemia  
- Tachycardia → compensatory response to low stroke volume  
- Cool, mottled knees → peripheral vasoconstriction (distributive or hypovolaemic shock)  
- High lactate → tissue hypoperfusion → anaerobic metabolism  
- Tachypnoea → compensation for metabolic acidosis (Kussmaul breathing)  
- Low JVP → hypovolaemia (or relative hypovolaemia in distribution)  
- Background pneumonia → likely septic shock (distributive)

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
1. **The “normotensive” shock**: Patient maintains BP via extreme vasoconstriction (e.g., young, fit) – only clue is tachycardia, low urine output, rising lactate. Don’t be falsely reassured by “normal” BP.
2. **The “warm” shock**: Early distributive (e.g., anaphylaxis, early sepsis) – skin pink and warm due to vasodilation, bounding pulses – can be misleading as they appear “well perfused” but are vasodilated and hypotensive.
3. **The bradycardic shock**: May occur with spinal shock (neurogenic) or beta-blocker overdose – heart rate doesn't rise; rely on other signs (hypotension, oliguria, lactate).

---
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
Shock is **inadequate tissue oxygen delivery** (DO₂) to meet metabolic demand. It is not just “low blood pressure” – hypotension is a late sign. The core problem is cellular hypoxia leading to anaerobic metabolism, lactic acidosis, and ultimately multi-organ failure if uncorrected.

**Why it matters:**
- High mortality (septic shock ~30-40%, cardiogenic shock ~50-80%)
- Reversible if caught early – every hour of hypotension increases mortality
- Common pitfall: treating number (BP) without addressing the underlying cause → worsens outcome
- Junior doctors are often first to see these patients; pattern recognition saves lives

---
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind Th:
**The story:**
Shock begins when the cardiovascular system fails to deliver enough oxygen to tissues. This can happen because:
- Pump failure (cardiogenic)
- Low filling volume (hypovolaemic)
- Widespread vasodilation (distributive)
- Obstruction to outflow (obstructive)

**Why each key sign happens:**
- **Hypotension** – In hypovolaemic/obstructive, loss of preload reduces stroke volume; in distributive, vasodilation drops systemic vascular resistance; in cardiogenic, pump failure reduces cardiac output.
- **Tachycardia** – Compensatory attempt by baroreceptors to maintain cardiac output (except in neurogenic or beta-blockade).
- **Cool, clammy skin** – Sympathetic-driven vasoconstriction (except in early distributive where skin is warm).
- **Oliguria** – Renal autoregulation lost when mean arterial pressure (MAP) < 65 mmHg; kidneys prioritise perfusion to heart/brain.
- **Lactic acidosis** – Cellular hypoxia → pyruvate shunted to lactate; also due to reduced hepatic clearance.
- **Confusion / reduced GCS** – Brain perfusion dependent on MAP; also direct effect of acidosis and cytokines.

**Connecting to pattern recognition cues:**
- In the vignette, low JVP points to hypovolaemia (or relative hypovolaemia in vasodilation). Cool skin suggests compensation – if warm, think distributive.
- Lactate > 2 mmol/L is the gold-mineral clue that tissue hypoperfusion is present.

---
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Hypovolaemic** | Haemorrhage (trauma, GI bleed, AAA rupture) | Blood in drains, melena, hypotension after injury |
| Hypovolaemic | Dehydration (vomiting, diarrhoea, burns) | Poor skin turgor, dry mucus membranes, high haematocrit |
| **Distributive** | Sepsis (most common cause of shock overall) | Fever, infection source, high lactate, warm peripheries early |
| Distributive | Anaphylaxis | Urticaria, wheeze, hives, sudden onset after drug/food/sting |
| Distributive | Neurogenic (spinal cord injury, high spinal anaesthesia) | Bradycardia, warm dry skin, hypotension with no compensatory tachycardia |
| Distributive | Adrenal crisis | Hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia, hyperpigmentation, refractory hypotension |
| **Cardiogenic** | Acute MI (especially LV failure) | Chest pain, ECG changes, raised troponin, pulmonary oedema |
| Cardiogenic | Arrhythmia (VT, AF with rapid rate) | Palpitations, irregular pulse, ECG arrhythmia |
| Cardiogenic | Valvular rupture / acute MR | New systolic murmur, flash pulmonary oedema, myocardial infarction |
| **Obstructive** | Tension pneumothorax | Tracheal deviation, absent breath sounds, hyper-resonance, distended neck veins |
| Obstructive | Cardiac tamponade | Beck’s triad (hypotension, muffled heart sounds, distended neck veins), pulsus paradoxus |
| Obstructive | Massive pulmonary embolism | Dyspnoea, hypoxia, pleuritic pain, signs of DVT, acute right heart strain on ECG (S1Q3T3) |

**Most common cause overall:** Distributive shock due to sepsis (septic shock).

---
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria:**
- **Shock** is a clinical syndrome; no single score is diagnostic.
- **Septic shock** (Sepsis-3): sepsis (qSOFA ≥ 2 OR organ dysfunction) + vasopressor requirement to maintain MAP ≥ 65 + lactate > 2 mmol/L despite adequate fluid resuscitation.
- **Cardiogenic shock**: systolic BP < 90 for > 30 min + signs of end-organ hypoperfusion + evidence of cardiac cause (ECG, echo, troponin).

**Key tests in ALL shock patients:**

| Test | What it shows | Why order | Interpretation pattern |
|------|---------------|-----------|------------------------|
| **Lactate** | Tissue hypoperfusion | Core marker; baseline and trend | > 2 = shock; rising despite Rx = worsening; falling = response |
| **Arterial blood gas (ABG)** | Acidosis (metabolic), PaO₂, PaCO₂, base deficit, lactate | Confirm hypoperfusion, guide ventilation, detect lactic acidosis | High lactate, low pH, high base deficit → anaerobic metabolism |
| **FBC / U&E / creatinine** | Hb, WBC, renal function, potassium | Detect anaemia (hypovolaemic), leucocytosis (sepsis), acute kidney injury (AKI) | High creatinine = renal hypoperfusion; low Hb = haemorrhage |
| **Chest X-ray** | Pneumothorax, pneumonia, pulmonary oedema, widened mediastinum | Identify obstructive and cardiogenic causes | Opacity = pneumonia; fluid = pulmonary oedema; air = pneumothorax |
| **ECG** | Ischaemia, arrhythmia, right heart strain | Rule out cardiogenic (STEMI, VT) or PE (S1Q3T3) | ST elevation = MI; atrial fibrillation = possible cardiogenic; RV strain = massive PE |
| **Echocardiogram (bedside)** | LV function, RV size, tamponade, valve abnormality | Differentiate cardiogenic from obstructive/distributive | Poor LV = cardiogenic; RV dilated + septal flattening = PE; pericardial effusion = tamponade |
| **Blood cultures** | Pathogen | If sepsis → target antibiotics | Positive = septic shock |
| **Coagulation screen** | DIC (prolonged PT/APTT, low fibrinogen) | If septic/obstructive shock → monitor for DIC | Rising INR/APTT + falling platelets = DIC |

**Single finding that confirms shock:** **Lactate > 2 mmol/L** in the right clinical context (hypotension + end-organ dysfunction). However, normal lactate does NOT rule out early shock (e.g., after fluids, or late stages with liver failure).

**Single finding that rules out hypoperfusion:** Normal lactate (< 2) + normal base deficit + normal urine output + stable GCS.

---
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour)** – ABCDE approach, treat the cause while supporting circulation.

1. **Airway / Breathing:** O₂ to target SpO₂ ≥ 94% (or > 88% if COPD with CO₂ retention). If airway compromised → call anaesthetics. Obtain IV/IO access (two large bore if haemorrhage).
2. **Fluids:** 30 mL/kg crystalloid (e.g., Hartmann’s) within 30 min for suspected septic/hypovolaemic shock **unless** cardiogenic/obstructive. In haemorrhage, give blood (O-negative, then cross-matched) early.
3. **Vasopressors:** If MAP < 65 after initial fluid bolus, start **norepinephrine** 0.05-0.5 µg/kg/min IV through central line (or peripheral if immediate). Titrate to MAP ≥ 65.
4. **Identify cause:**
   - **Septic shock:** Take blood cultures, start broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV q6h + gentamicin 5-7 mg/kg IV once, or local protocol).
   - **Cardiogenic:** Avoid fluid bolus; give diuretics if pulmonary oedema (furosemide 40 mg IV) and consult cardiology for revascularisation.
   - **Obstructive:** Needle decompression for tension pneumothorax (14G cannula 2nd intercostal space midclavicular line); pericardiocentesis for tamponade; thrombolysis for massive PE if no contraindication.
   - **Anaphylaxis:** IM adrenaline 0.5 mg (0.5 mL of 1:1000) anterolateral thigh; repeat every 5 min if no response.
   - **Neurogenic:** Fluids, atropine if bradycardia, and consider vasopressors (phenylephrine or norepinephrine).

**Ward (24-72 hours) – ongoing care:**
- Monitor lactate clearance (target > 20% reduction in 6 hours)
- Check urine output hourly (target > 0.5 mL/kg/hr)
- Adjust vasopressor to wean off as tolerated
- Source control (drain abscess, remove infected line, surgery for bleeding)
- In septic shock: recheck cultures, narrow antibiotics per sensitivities; consider hydrocortisone 50 mg IV q6h if vasopressor dependent
- In cardiogenic: consider inotropes (dobutamine 2.5-20 µg/kg/min) if low cardiac output despite vasopressors
- Nutrition, VTE prophylaxis, stress ulcer prophylaxis

**Long-term:**
- Treat underlying condition (e.g., PCI for MI, valve surgery, chronic infection)
- Survivors need rehab, follow-up for organ recovery (renal, cardiac)

**What NOT to do:**
- Do NOT use only fluids for cardiogenic shock → worsens pulmonary oedema
- Do NOT give large fluid volumes in obstructive shock without first relieving obstruction
- Do NOT use dopamine as first-line vasopressor (more arrhythmias) – norepinephrine is superior
- Do NOT wait for CVP or echo before starting vasopressors in severe hypotension – MAP > 65 NOW

---
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Septic Shock (Distributive) | Cardiogenic Shock | Hypovolaemic Shock | Obstructive Shock |
|---------|-----------------------------|-------------------|--------------------|-------------------|
| **Pain** | Often none; can have abdominal, limb pain (source) | Chest pain (MI), dyspnoea | Often none (but trauma pain, abdominal pain) | Pleuritic (PE), chest tightness (tamponade), pleuritic+tracheal (pneumothorax) |
| **Exam** | Fever or hypothermia; warm then cool; tachycardia; +/- source | Cool, pale, pulmonary oedema (crackles), S3 gallop, raised JVP | Cool, clammy, low JVP, dry mucous membranes, tachycardia | Tachy/brady, distended neck veins (except tension pneumothorax: tracheal deviation, absent breath sounds); muffled heart sounds (tamponade); RV heave (PE) |
| **ECG** | Non-specific (sinus tach) | STEMI, arrhythmia, new Q waves, ST depression | Non-specific (sinus tach) | PE: S1Q3T3, RV strain; Tamponade: low voltage, electrical alternans |
| **Labs** | High lactate, raised WBC, rise in creatinine, CRP; positive cultures | High troponin, BNP; possibly raised lactate | High lactate if late; metabolic acidosis; raised urea/creatinine (pre-renal) | High D-dimer (PE); possible raised troponin (if strain); lactate may be high |
| **Key differentiator** | Response to IV fluids, source of infection | ECG + echo + troponin + pulmonary oedema | Low JVP + high haematocrit (if haemorrhage) | Echo (tamponade, RV strain, pneumothorax on CXR) |

---
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**Irreversible shock / Multi-organ failure**
- **How it presents:** Progressive hypotension despite vasopressors, anuria, acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS), hepatic failure (jaundice, coagulopathy), DIC.
- **Key signs:** Lactate > 10, base deficit > -10, INR > 2, platelets < 50, creatinine > 300.
- **What to do:** ICU admission, full organ support (ventilation, CVVHDF, blood products). Prognosis poor.

**Acute Kidney Injury (AKI)**
- **Presentation:** Oliguria, rising creatinine. Late → anuria, hyperkalaemia, fluid overload.
- **Key signs:** Cr rise > 1.5x baseline, urine output < 0.5 mL/kg/hr.
- **Action:** Optimise perfusion, stop nephrotoxins, consider RRT if refractory.

**Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS)**
- **Presentation:** Severe hypoxemia, bilateral infiltrates on CXR, within first week of shock.
- **Key signs:** PaO₂/FiO₂ < 200 on vent support.
- **Action:** Lung-protective ventilation, prone positioning, treat cause.

**Limb Ischaemia (in vasopressor overuse or DIC)**
- **Presentation:** Distal digits (fingers, toes) become white, painful, then necrotic.
- **Key signs:** Capillary refill > 3 sec, loss of pulse, mottling.
- **Action:** Reduce vasopressors, consider surgical embolectomy if embolic, but often iatrogenic.

**Late presentation (untreated shock) – exam stem:**
- Patient found in asystole or bradyarrhythmia; lactate > 15; pH < 7.0.
- Often tests recognition: “What is the most likely complication of prolonged septic shock?” → Multi-organ failure / DIC.

---
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonic for causes of shock:**
**CHORD** – Cardiogenic, Hypovolaemic, Obstructive, Reactive (distributive), Endocrine (adrenal crisis, thyrotoxicosis).

**Mnemonic for early management – THE FAST HOURS** (adapted from Surviving Sepsis Campaign):
- **T** – Triage (identify shock)
- **H** – Huddle (call senior, team)
- **E** – Establish IV access
- **F** – Fluids (30 mL/kg, but beware cardiogenic)
- **A** – Antibiotics (sepsis) / Analgesia
- **S** – Source control
- **T** – Track lactate, urine output, MAP
- **H** – Hemodynamics (vasopressors if MAP < 65)
- **O** – Oxygen
- **U** – Urgent echo / ultrasound
- **R** – Review response (repeat lactate, BP)
- **S** – Senior review / escalation

**5-7 Clinical Pearls:**
1. **Lactate is the canary in the coal mine.** A high lactate with a normal BP = shock (compensated). Wait for hypotension and you’ve lost time.
2. **The JVP is the clue.** Low JVP = hypovolaemia (or relative hypovolaemia in early distributive); high JVP = cardiogenic / obstructive. Simple bedside rule that changes management.
3. **Warm shock is still shock.** A patient with bounding pulses, pink skin, but MAP 60 and lactate 4 is in distributive shock. Do not be fooled by “good perfusion”.
4. **Cardiogenic shock does not love fluids.** Give small (250 mL) cautious boluses only if hypotension; if JVP is high, stop and give diuretics/inotropes.
5. **Norepinephrine is first-line for all types** except in some neurogenic (phenylephrine) and anaphylaxis (adrenaline). Dopamine is out.
6. **Tension pneumothorax needs needle decompression — not a chest drain first.** The needle buys time; drain later.
7. **Pericardial tamponade — don’t wait for Beck’s triad** (all three are present in only 30%). If hypotension + distended neck veins + muffled heart sounds, do a bedside echo.

**Common mistakes to avoid:**
- Ignoring the lactate because the BP is ‘normal’.
- Giving 2-3 litres of crystalloid to a cardiogenic shock patient → precipitates pulmonary oedema.
- Not taking blood cultures before antibiotics → forever uncertain of the pathogen.
- Starting vasopressors too late because ‘we haven’t finished fluids yet’ – if MAP < 65 after 10 min of fluids, start noradrenaline.

---
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call a senior/ICU/crash team immediately:**
- Any patient in shock (clinical diagnosis) → call medical registrar / ICU outreach
- MAP < 60 despite 30 mL/kg of fluids + starting noradrenaline
- GCS ≤ 12 or falling
- Respiratory distress (RR > 30, SpO₂ < 90% on O₂)
- Lactate > 4 mmol/L (or rising despite initial treatment)
- NEWSed **≥ 7**: score combines RR, SpO₂, BP, HR, consciousness, temperature. Any single parameter scoring 3 (e.g., systolic BP ≤ 90) triggers immediate escalation.
- ECG showing STEMI, VT, or high-grade AV block
- Cardiac arrest or peri-arrest rhythm
- Suspected tension pneumothorax, cardiac tamponade, or massive PE with shock

**Specific thresholds:**
- **NEWS2** ≥ 5 → urgent clinical review; ≥ 7 → emergency response (critical care)
- **Lactate** > 2 mmol/L → escalate; > 4 → ICU review
- **GCS** ≤ 13 → consider airway protection, CT head, senior review
- **Urine output** < 0.5 mL/kg/hr for > 2 hours → escalate
- **Arterial pH** < 7.2 → consider ICU for metabolic support

**Always escalate if:**
- You feel the patient is deteriorating
- You haven’t identified the cause within 15 min of recognising shock
- The patient needs inotropes or central access beyond your competence

**Bottom line:** Shock is a time-critical emergency. Recognise early (> lactate), treat aggressively (fluids + cause + vasopressors), and escalate early. Don’t wait for the BP to crash before you call for help.

rawMarkdown:
## Shock — Overview

**One-liner:** Acute circulatory failure causing inadequate tissue oxygen delivery; life-threatening, common in all hospital settings, requires immediate recognition and cause-specific resuscitation.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic Vignette:**
A 68-year-old man admitted for pneumonia on Day 2 becomes confused, oliguric, and hypotensive (BP 82/45). He is tachycardic (HR 115), has cool, mottled knees, and a lactate of 5.1 mmol/L. His respiratory rate is 28, and his oxygen saturations are 88% on 4L. His JVP is low.

**Dissecting the vignette:**
- Confusion → reduced cerebral perfusion / metabolic acidosis  
- Oliguria → reduced renal perfusion pressure  
- Hypotension → loss of vascular tone +/or hypovolaemia  
- Tachycardia → compensatory response to low stroke volume  
- Cool, mottled knees → peripheral vasoconstriction (distributive or hypovolaemic shock)  
- High lactate → tissue hypoperfusion → anaerobic metabolism  
- Tachypnoea → compensation for metabolic acidosis (Kussmaul breathing)  
- Low JVP → hypovolaemia (or relative hypovolaemia in distribution)  
- Background pneumonia → likely septic shock (distributive)

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
1. **The “normotensive” shock**: Patient maintains BP via extreme vasoconstriction (e.g., young, fit) – only clue is tachycardia, low urine output, rising lactate. Don’t be falsely reassured by “normal” BP.
2. **The “warm” shock**: Early distributive (e.g., anaphylaxis, early sepsis) – skin pink and warm due to vasodilation, bounding pulses – can be misleading as they appear “well perfused” but are vasodilated and hypotensive.
3. **The bradycardic shock**: May occur with spinal shock (neurogenic) or beta-blocker overdose – heart rate doesn't rise; rely on other signs (hypotension, oliguria, lactate).

---

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

Shock is **inadequate tissue oxygen delivery** (DO₂) to meet metabolic demand. It is not just “low blood pressure” – hypotension is a late sign. The core problem is cellular hypoxia leading to anaerobic metabolism, lactic acidosis, and ultimately multi-organ failure if uncorrected.

**Why it matters:**
- High mortality (septic shock ~30-40%, cardiogenic shock ~50-80%)
- Reversible if caught early – every hour of hypotension increases mortality
- Common pitfall: treating number (BP) without addressing the underlying cause → worsens outcome
- Junior doctors are often first to see these patients; pattern recognition saves lives

---

### 3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind The Findings

**The story:**
Shock begins when the cardiovascular system fails to deliver enough oxygen to tissues. This can happen because:
- Pump failure (cardiogenic)
- Low filling volume (hypovolaemic)
- Widespread vasodilation (distributive)
- Obstruction to outflow (obstructive)

**Why each key sign happens:**
- **Hypotension** – In hypovolaemic/obstructive, loss of preload reduces stroke volume; in distributive, vasodilation drops systemic vascular resistance; in cardiogenic, pump failure reduces cardiac output.
- **Tachycardia** – Compensatory attempt by baroreceptors to maintain cardiac output (except in neurogenic or beta-blockade).
- **Cool, clammy skin** – Sympathetic-driven vasoconstriction (except in early distributive where skin is warm).
- **Oliguria** – Renal autoregulation lost when mean arterial pressure (MAP) < 65 mmHg; kidneys prioritise perfusion to heart/brain.
- **Lactic acidosis** – Cellular hypoxia → pyruvate shunted to lactate; also due to reduced hepatic clearance.
- **Confusion / reduced GCS** – Brain perfusion dependent on MAP; also direct effect of acidosis and cytokines.

**Connecting to pattern recognition cues:**
- In the vignette, low JVP points to hypovolaemia (or relative hypovolaemia in vasodilation). Cool skin suggests compensation – if warm, think distributive.
- Lactate > 2 mmol/L is the gold-mineral clue that tissue hypoperfusion is present.

---

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Hypovolaemic** | Haemorrhage (trauma, GI bleed, AAA rupture) | Blood in drains, melena, hypotension after injury |
| Hypovolaemic | Dehydration (vomiting, diarrhoea, burns) | Poor skin turgor, dry mucus membranes, high haematocrit |
| **Distributive** | Sepsis (most common cause of shock overall) | Fever, infection source, high lactate, warm peripheries early |
| Distributive | Anaphylaxis | Urticaria, wheeze, hives, sudden onset after drug/food/sting |
| Distributive | Neurogenic (spinal cord injury, high spinal anaesthesia) | Bradycardia, warm dry skin, hypotension with no compensatory tachycardia |
| Distributive | Adrenal crisis | Hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia, hyperpigmentation, refractory hypotension |
| **Cardiogenic** | Acute MI (especially LV failure) | Chest pain, ECG changes, raised troponin, pulmonary oedema |
| Cardiogenic | Arrhythmia (VT, AF with rapid rate) | Palpitations, irregular pulse, ECG arrhythmia |
| Cardiogenic | Valvular rupture / acute MR | New systolic murmur, flash pulmonary oedema, myocardial infarction |
| **Obstructive** | Tension pneumothorax | Tracheal deviation, absent breath sounds, hyper-resonance, distended neck veins |
| Obstructive | Cardiac tamponade | Beck’s triad (hypotension, muffled heart sounds, distended neck veins), pulsus paradoxus |
| Obstructive | Massive pulmonary embolism | Dyspnoea, hypoxia, pleuritic pain, signs of DVT, acute right heart strain on ECG (S1Q3T3) |

**Most common cause overall:** Distributive shock due to sepsis (septic shock).

---

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria:**
- **Shock** is a clinical syndrome; no single score is diagnostic.
- **Septic shock** (Sepsis-3): sepsis (qSOFA ≥ 2 OR organ dysfunction) + vasopressor requirement to maintain MAP ≥ 65 + lactate > 2 mmol/L despite adequate fluid resuscitation.
- **Cardiogenic shock**: systolic BP < 90 for > 30 min + signs of end-organ hypoperfusion + evidence of cardiac cause (ECG, echo, troponin).

**Key tests in ALL shock patients:**

| Test | What it shows | Why order | Interpretation pattern |
|------|---------------|-----------|------------------------|
| **Lactate** | Tissue hypoperfusion | Core marker; baseline and trend | > 2 = shock; rising despite Rx = worsening; falling = response |
| **Arterial blood gas (ABG)** | Acidosis (metabolic), PaO₂, PaCO₂, base deficit, lactate | Confirm hypoperfusion, guide ventilation, detect lactic acidosis | High lactate, low pH, high base deficit → anaerobic metabolism |
| **FBC / U&E / creatinine** | Hb, WBC, renal function, potassium | Detect anaemia (hypovolaemic), leucocytosis (sepsis), acute kidney injury (AKI) | High creatinine = renal hypoperfusion; low Hb = haemorrhage |
| **Chest X-ray** | Pneumothorax, pneumonia, pulmonary oedema, widened mediastinum | Identify obstructive and cardiogenic causes | Opacity = pneumonia; fluid = pulmonary oedema; air = pneumothorax |
| **ECG** | Ischaemia, arrhythmia, right heart strain | Rule out cardiogenic (STEMI, VT) or PE (S1Q3T3) | ST elevation = MI; atrial fibrillation = possible cardiogenic; RV strain = massive PE |
| **Echocardiogram (bedside)** | LV function, RV size, tamponade, valve abnormality | Differentiate cardiogenic from obstructive/distributive | Poor LV = cardiogenic; RV dilated + septal flattening = PE; pericardial effusion = tamponade |
| **Blood cultures** | Pathogen | If sepsis → target antibiotics | Positive = septic shock |
| **Coagulation screen** | DIC (prolonged PT/APTT, low fibrinogen) | If septic/obstructive shock → monitor for DIC | Rising INR/APTT + falling platelets = DIC |

**Single finding that confirms shock:** **Lactate > 2 mmol/L** in the right clinical context (hypotension + end-organ dysfunction). However, normal lactate does NOT rule out early shock (e.g., after fluids, or late stages with liver failure).

**Single finding that rules out hypoperfusion:** Normal lactate (< 2) + normal base deficit + normal urine output + stable GCS.

---

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour)** – ABCDE approach, treat the cause while supporting circulation.

1. **Airway / Breathing:** O₂ to target SpO₂ ≥ 94% (or > 88% if COPD with CO₂ retention). If airway compromised → call anaesthetics. Obtain IV/IO access (two large bore if haemorrhage).
2. **Fluids:** 30 mL/kg crystalloid (e.g., Hartmann’s) within 30 min for suspected septic/hypovolaemic shock **unless** cardiogenic/obstructive. In haemorrhage, give blood (O-negative, then cross-matched) early.
3. **Vasopressors:** If MAP < 65 after initial fluid bolus, start **norepinephrine** 0.05-0.5 µg/kg/min IV through central line (or peripheral if immediate). Titrate to MAP ≥ 65.
4. **Identify cause:**
   - **Septic shock:** Take blood cultures, start broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV q6h + gentamicin 5-7 mg/kg IV once, or local protocol).
   - **Cardiogenic:** Avoid fluid bolus; give diuretics if pulmonary oedema (furosemide 40 mg IV) and consult cardiology for revascularisation.
   - **Obstructive:** Needle decompression for tension pneumothorax (14G cannula 2nd intercostal space midclavicular line); pericardiocentesis for tamponade; thrombolysis for massive PE if no contraindication.
   - **Anaphylaxis:** IM adrenaline 0.5 mg (0.5 mL of 1:1000) anterolateral thigh; repeat every 5 min if no response.
   - **Neurogenic:** Fluids, atropine if bradycardia, and consider vasopressors (phenylephrine or norepinephrine).

**Ward (24-72 hours) – ongoing care:**
- Monitor lactate clearance (target > 20% reduction in 6 hours)
- Check urine output hourly (target > 0.5 mL/kg/hr)
- Adjust vasopressor to wean off as tolerated
- Source control (drain abscess, remove infected line, surgery for bleeding)
- In septic shock: recheck cultures, narrow antibiotics per sensitivities; consider hydrocortisone 50 mg IV q6h if vasopressor dependent
- In cardiogenic: consider inotropes (dobutamine 2.5-20 µg/kg/min) if low cardiac output despite vasopressors
- Nutrition, VTE prophylaxis, stress ulcer prophylaxis

**Long-term:**
- Treat underlying condition (e.g., PCI for MI, valve surgery, chronic infection)
- Survivors need rehab, follow-up for organ recovery (renal, cardiac)

**What NOT to do:**
- Do NOT use only fluids for cardiogenic shock → worsens pulmonary oedema
- Do NOT give large fluid volumes in obstructive shock without first relieving obstruction
- Do NOT use dopamine as first-line vasopressor (more arrhythmias) – norepinephrine is superior
- Do NOT wait for CVP or echo before starting vasopressors in severe hypotension – MAP > 65 NOW

---

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Septic Shock (Distributive) | Cardiogenic Shock | Hypovolaemic Shock | Obstructive Shock |
|---------|-----------------------------|-------------------|--------------------|-------------------|
| **Pain** | Often none; can have abdominal, limb pain (source) | Chest pain (MI), dyspnoea | Often none (but trauma pain, abdominal pain) | Pleuritic (PE), chest tightness (tamponade), pleuritic+tracheal (pneumothorax) |
| **Exam** | Fever or hypothermia; warm then cool; tachycardia; +/- source | Cool, pale, pulmonary oedema (crackles), S3 gallop, raised JVP | Cool, clammy, low JVP, dry mucous membranes, tachycardia | Tachy/brady, distended neck veins (except tension pneumothorax: tracheal deviation, absent breath sounds); muffled heart sounds (tamponade); RV heave (PE) |
| **ECG** | Non-specific (sinus tach) | STEMI, arrhythmia, new Q waves, ST depression | Non-specific (sinus tach) | PE: S1Q3T3, RV strain; Tamponade: low voltage, electrical alternans |
| **Labs** | High lactate, raised WBC, rise in creatinine, CRP; positive cultures | High troponin, BNP; possibly raised lactate | High lactate if late; metabolic acidosis; raised urea/creatinine (pre-renal) | High D-dimer (PE); possible raised troponin (if strain); lactate may be high |
| **Key differentiator** | Response to IV fluids, source of infection | ECG + echo + troponin + pulmonary oedema | Low JVP + high haematocrit (if haemorrhage) | Echo (tamponade, RV strain, pneumothorax on CXR) |

---

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

**Irreversible shock / Multi-organ failure**
- **How it presents:** Progressive hypotension despite vasopressors, anuria, acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS), hepatic failure (jaundice, coagulopathy), DIC.
- **Key signs:** Lactate > 10, base deficit > -10, INR > 2, platelets < 50, creatinine > 300.
- **What to do:** ICU admission, full organ support (ventilation, CVVHDF, blood products). Prognosis poor.

**Acute Kidney Injury (AKI)**
- **Presentation:** Oliguria, rising creatinine. Late → anuria, hyperkalaemia, fluid overload.
- **Key signs:** Cr rise > 1.5x baseline, urine output < 0.5 mL/kg/hr.
- **Action:** Optimise perfusion, stop nephrotoxins, consider RRT if refractory.

**Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS)**
- **Presentation:** Severe hypoxemia, bilateral infiltrates on CXR, within first week of shock.
- **Key signs:** PaO₂/FiO₂ < 200 on vent support.
- **Action:** Lung-protective ventilation, prone positioning, treat cause.

**Limb Ischaemia (in vasopressor overuse or DIC)**
- **Presentation:** Distal digits (fingers, toes) become white, painful, then necrotic.
- **Key signs:** Capillary refill > 3 sec, loss of pulse, mottling.
- **Action:** Reduce vasopressors, consider surgical embolectomy if embolic, but often iatrogenic.

**Late presentation (untreated shock) – exam stem:**
- Patient found in asystole or bradyarrhythmia; lactate > 15; pH < 7.0.
- Often tests recognition: “What is the most likely complication of prolonged septic shock?” → Multi-organ failure / DIC.

---

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonic for causes of shock:**
**CHORD** – Cardiogenic, Hypovolaemic, Obstructive, Reactive (distributive), Endocrine (adrenal crisis, thyrotoxicosis).

**Mnemonic for early management – THE FAST HOURS** (adapted from Surviving Sepsis Campaign):
- **T** – Triage (identify shock)
- **H** – Huddle (call senior, team)
- **E** – Establish IV access
- **F** – Fluids (30 mL/kg, but beware cardiogenic)
- **A** – Antibiotics (sepsis) / Analgesia
- **S** – Source control
- **T** – Track lactate, urine output, MAP
- **H** – Hemodynamics (vasopressors if MAP < 65)
- **O** – Oxygen
- **U** – Urgent echo / ultrasound
- **R** – Review response (repeat lactate, BP)
- **S** – Senior review / escalation

**5-7 Clinical Pearls:**
1. **Lactate is the canary in the coal mine.** A high lactate with a normal BP = shock (compensated). Wait for hypotension and you’ve lost time.
2. **The JVP is the clue.** Low JVP = hypovolaemia (or relative hypovolaemia in early distributive); high JVP = cardiogenic / obstructive. Simple bedside rule that changes management.
3. **Warm shock is still shock.** A patient with bounding pulses, pink skin, but MAP 60 and lactate 4 is in distributive shock. Do not be fooled by “good perfusion”.
4. **Cardiogenic shock does not love fluids.** Give small (250 mL) cautious boluses only if hypotension; if JVP is high, stop and give diuretics/inotropes.
5. **Norepinephrine is first-line for all types** except in some neurogenic (phenylephrine) and anaphylaxis (adrenaline). Dopamine is out.
6. **Tension pneumothorax needs needle decompression — not a chest drain first.** The needle buys time; drain later.
7. **Pericardial tamponade — don’t wait for Beck’s triad** (all three are present in only 30%). If hypotension + distended neck veins + muffled heart sounds, do a bedside echo.

**Common mistakes to avoid:**
- Ignoring the lactate because the BP is ‘normal’.
- Giving 2-3 litres of crystalloid to a cardiogenic shock patient → precipitates pulmonary oedema.
- Not taking blood cultures before antibiotics → forever uncertain of the pathogen.
- Starting vasopressors too late because ‘we haven’t finished fluids yet’ – if MAP < 65 after 10 min of fluids, start noradrenaline.

---

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call a senior/ICU/crash team immediately:**
- Any patient in shock (clinical diagnosis) → call medical registrar / ICU outreach
- MAP < 60 despite 30 mL/kg of fluids + starting noradrenaline
- GCS ≤ 12 or falling
- Respiratory distress (RR > 30, SpO₂ < 90% on O₂)
- Lactate > 4 mmol/L (or rising despite initial treatment)
- NEWSed **≥ 7**: score combines RR, SpO₂, BP, HR, consciousness, temperature. Any single parameter scoring 3 (e.g., systolic BP ≤ 90) triggers immediate escalation.
- ECG showing STEMI, VT, or high-grade AV block
- Cardiac arrest or peri-arrest rhythm
- Suspected tension pneumothorax, cardiac tamponade, or massive PE with shock

**Specific thresholds:**
- **NEWS2** ≥ 5 → urgent clinical review; ≥ 7 → emergency response (critical care)
- **Lactate** > 2 mmol/L → escalate; > 4 → ICU review
- **GCS** ≤ 13 → consider airway protection, CT head, senior review
- **Urine output** < 0.5 mL/kg/hr for > 2 hours → escalate
- **Arterial pH** < 7.2 → consider ICU for metabolic support

**Always escalate if:**
- You feel the patient is deteriorating
- You haven’t identified the cause within 15 min of recognising shock
- The patient needs inotropes or central access beyond your competence

**Bottom line:** Shock is a time-critical emergency. Recognise early (> lactate), treat aggressively (fluids + cause + vasopressors), and escalate early. Don’t wait for the BP to crash before you call for help.
